title: Company Picnic

jokes.text: Sure, I'll give him the message when he gets up... Gets back.
jokes.setup: Erin answers phone professionally about Michael
jokes.punchline: Accidentally reveals he's sleeping by saying 'gets up' before correcting to 'gets back'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Erin - well-meaning but accidentally revealing. The correction makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Michael Scott's office, this is Erin speaking. How may I direct your call?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh, he's just... he gets up at 6 AM every morning. Gets back to work right away.

jokes.text: Michael ate an entire family-sized chicken pot pie for lunch, and then he fell asleep.
jokes.setup: Jim explains Michael's absence
jokes.punchline: The escalating specificity from 'chicken pot pie' to 'entire' to 'family-sized' building to the ridiculous conclusion
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure with perfect comedic timing in the repetition and build-up. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael's not here. He's at home making chicken pot pie.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Not just any chicken pot pie. An entire chicken pot pie.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A family-sized chicken pot pie.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: For one person.

jokes.text: I don't condone leaving early. But I have an appointment with the horse doctor. How that horse became a doctor, I don't know.
jokes.setup: Dwight mentions horse doctor appointment
jokes.punchline: Pretends to wonder how a horse became a doctor instead of clarifying it's a veterinarian
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - his literal-mindedness turned into deliberate misdirection. Highly quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have to leave early today. I have an appointment with my horse doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Your horse doctor?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How did a horse become a doctor?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't know, Jim. That's what I'm going to find out.

jokes.text: I'm kidding. He's just a regular doctor who shoots your horse in the head when its leg is broken.
jokes.setup: Dwight's fake-out about the horse doctor
jokes.punchline: The 'clarification' is even darker and more absurd than the original misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - the 'correction' is darker than the original joke. Peak Dwight absurdity with perfect delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm not a horse doctor. I'm a beet farmer.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh thank God.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm a physician. For horses. I perform euthanasia.

jokes.text: Like clockwork.
jokes.setup: Michael wakes up right at 5 PM as predicted
jokes.punchline: His satisfied reaction to perfectly timing his nap, oblivious to everyone's conspiracy
jokes.explanation: Michael's oblivious pride in his 'natural' timing while everyone manipulated clocks around him. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ahhhh! Five o'clock on the dot. Perfect nap. I am a nap machine.

jokes.text: Geography joke.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'You had to be there!' about something happening at the office
jokes.punchline: Calls it a 'geography joke' as if physical location was the punchline
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - misunderstanding what makes something location-dependent. Economical and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You had to be there!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's a geography joke.

jokes.text: See you all tamale.
jokes.setup: Michael saying goodbye
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'tomorrow' as 'tamale' (the food)
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael malapropism. Functional but not particularly creative or surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'll see you all tamale!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, tomorrow. I meant tomorrow.

jokes.text: Should I have left the car running?
jokes.setup: Jim and Pam plan to show faces briefly and leave
jokes.punchline: Jim's deadpan question about keeping car running for quick escape
jokes.explanation: Good setup for their reluctance about the picnic. Jim's dry delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So we're just going to show our faces and leave?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's the plan.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Should I keep the car running?

jokes.text: You don't grab these for balance.
jokes.setup: Explaining drunk guy's excuse for inappropriate touching
jokes.punchline: Pam's matter-of-fact delivery while presumably gesturing to her breasts
jokes.explanation: Pam's deadpan delivery of the obvious makes this work. Good visual comedy implied.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: He said he thought they were real.

jokes.text: You think the EPA would ever allow that much deet?
jokes.setup: Discussion of homemade SPF/insect repellant combination
jokes.punchline: Angela's concern about EPA regulations for homemade bug spray
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela - treating homemade bug spray like industrial chemical manufacturing. Character-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: You can't just make your own bug spray at home. The EPA has very strict regulations about that sort of thing.

jokes.text: I heard him asking for a shoe that could increase his speed and not leave any tracks.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining how he met his 'best friend' Rolph
jokes.punchline: Rolph was apparently shopping for cartoon roadrunner/criminal shoes
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd detail that reveals both Michael's gullibility and Rolph's suspicious nature.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I met Rolph at the shoe store. He was looking at these shoes, and I said, 'Those are some pretty sweet shoes.' And he said, 'Yeah, they're for running away from the cops.' And I was like, 'That's awesome, we're best friends now.'

jokes.text: The combinations are endless.
jokes.setup: Holly offering lemonade to Jim and Pam, or one of them, or both
jokes.punchline: Dramatically overstating the complexity of serving two drinks to two people
jokes.explanation: Holly's overthinking a simple drink order. Shows her quirky analytical mind similar to Michael's.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Would you guys like some lemonade?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Sure, thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, that sounds great.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Okay, so Jim gets one lemonade, and Pam gets another lemonade. That's two lemonades total. One for each person. I'll need two cups, two sets of ice, two spoons for stirring... This is very complicated. Very complicated logistics here.

jokes.text: You can go to hell. I'm kidding! Sure, I will get you the best iced tea in the world.
jokes.setup: AJ asks for iced tea instead of lemonade
jokes.punchline: Holly's extreme overreaction followed by equally extreme accommodation
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Holly's dramatic swing from hostility to over-the-top helpfulness.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Iced tea? ICED TEA?! Do you know what iced tea does to the human body? It's basically liquid Satan!

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: But you know what? I'm going to make you the best iced tea this office has ever seen. I'm talking fresh-brewed, perfectly steeped, ice cubes carved from the tears of angels.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: I'm going to drive to three different stores to find the exact tea leaves you need. I'll learn Mandarin if it helps me source the best ones.

jokes.text: You and I are soup snakes. That doesn't make any sense. We're soul mates.
jokes.setup: Michael rehearsing his speech about why he and Holly belong together
jokes.punchline: Accidentally says 'soup snakes' instead of 'soul mates' then corrects himself
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael malapropism with self-awareness. 'Soup snakes' is absurd enough to be memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We are soup snakes. I mean, soul mates.

jokes.text: Must be nice to get a rest from all your rest.
jokes.setup: Packer greeting Jim at the picnic
jokes.punchline: Implying Jim does nothing but rest at work
jokes.explanation: Typical Packer dig. In character but not particularly clever or original.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Hey Jim! How's it going? You look well-rested.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Thanks, Todd. Yeah, I guess I am.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Well, you should be. You've been napping at your desk all day.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What? No I haven't.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Jim, I've seen you. You come in, you sit down, you close your eyes. That's the job, right?

jokes.text: I shouldn't have to ask you to do that. You should just do it.
jokes.setup: Jim asks if Pam wants him to beat up Packer
jokes.punchline: Pam's matter-of-fact expectation that Jim should automatically know to fight for her honor
jokes.explanation: Good relationship humor with Pam's deadpan delivery selling the absurd expectation.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Do you want me to beat up Packer?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Well, yeah.

jokes.text: Well, I could have died, so...
jokes.setup: Andy mentions Jim's spider-in-the-mitt incident from last year
jokes.punchline: Pam's casual understatement about nearly dying from a spider
jokes.explanation: Pam's deadpan delivery of dramatic overstatement. Nice callback to establish history.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, that was just a little spider. I almost died.

jokes.text: You want her to set you up so you can spike it. I'm gonna put you next to Phyllis. She's the best setter on the team.
jokes.setup: Ryan asks to be on Erin's row
jokes.punchline: Andy completely misunderstands Ryan's romantic interest as volleyball strategy
jokes.explanation: Andy's obliviousness to Ryan's obvious flirting. Good misdirection with volleyball terminology.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Can I be on Erin's row?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, so you want to set with Erin. Smart. She's got great court vision.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: No, I just—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And her serve-receive is solid. You two will make a killer front line.

jokes.text: For us.
jokes.setup: Andy asks who Holly and AJ are designing a house for
jokes.punchline: Holly's simple answer creates awkward silence as Michael realizes they're serious
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - two words that devastate Michael. The silence after is the real punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Who are you guys designing a house for?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Us.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: ...What?

jokes.text: I'm designing a chair. It's part of your pants. You sit down, you're supported.
jokes.setup: Michael deflecting after Holly's house comment
jokes.punchline: Revives his old chair pants idea as desperate conversation filler
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Michael's desperate deflection, though the callback itself isn't particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? Chair pants. That's what I should be focusing on. Pants... made of chair. I could be a millionaire.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Michael, we were just talking about my house.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But think about it. You're sitting down all day at work. Why not be sitting... in your pants?

jokes.text: All right, fatty. I will do it.
jokes.setup: Kevin wants chair pants in size 34 waist
jokes.punchline: Michael casually calls Kevin 'fatty' while agreeing to help
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael casual cruelty. In character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I need chair pants in size 34 waist.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yeah, sure thing, fatty. I'll help you out.

jokes.text: You bet your fat ass we are.
jokes.setup: Someone questions if Michael and Holly will really do their presentation
jokes.punchline: Holly matches Michael's crude energy with her own fat joke
jokes.explanation: Shows Holly matching Michael's inappropriate humor. Good character development but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Of course we will. Michael's not gonna back out on me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's right. We're gonna knock 'em dead.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Yeah, we are. And if anyone has a problem with it, they can take it up with me. I may be small, but I'm scrappy. And I've got more moves than a... than a fat guy at an all-you-can-eat buffet.

jokes.text: Have ya hoyd the news? Extry, extry! Read all about it! Newspapers for sale!
jokes.setup: Holly and Michael rehearsing their historical presentation
jokes.punchline: Holly's over-the-top old-timey newsboy impression
jokes.explanation: Generic period comedy accent. Shows their amateur theatrical approach but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Extra! Extra! Read all about it! The British are coming! The British are coming!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Holly, that's... that's really good.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: I've been practicing. Extra! Extra!

jokes.text: Sir... with the glasses, are you literally blind?
jokes.setup: Someone wearing sunglasses at the volleyball game
jokes.punchline: Michael's inappropriate direct question about blindness to a stranger
jokes.explanation: Peak Michael inappropriateness. The specific phrasing 'literally blind' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Are you blind? Is that why you're wearing those sunglasses?

jokes.text: These are expensive Ray-Bans, jackass.
jokes.setup: Michael's inappropriate blindness question
jokes.punchline: The man's justified irritation and brand-name defense
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response to Michael's absurdity. The Ray-Bans detail adds specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: So you're blind?

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo employee
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Since birth?

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo employee
dialogue.line: No, I lost my sight in an accident.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's terrible. So you can't see anything at all?

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo employee
dialogue.line: Look, I don't know what you want from me, but I need to get back to work.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm just curious — do you use a seeing eye dog?

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo employee
dialogue.line: It's a German Shepherd. And it's a service dog, not a 'seeing eye dog.'

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: What's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo employee
dialogue.line: That's a brand name. Like Kleenex. It's not accurate.

jokes.text: Are... you... blind? Can you see things with your eyeballs?
jokes.setup: Kevin continues Michael's inappropriate line of questioning
jokes.punchline: Kevin's even more direct and childish phrasing about eyeball function
jokes.explanation: Kevin's childlike directness escalates Michael's inappropriate question perfectly. 'Eyeballs' is a great word choice.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: So like, when you look at something, does the picture go into your eyeball and then your brain looks at the picture? Or does your brain go out into your eyeball to look at the picture?

jokes.text: I am better than this.
jokes.setup: Coach giving pep talk about being better than their current performance
jokes.punchline: Ryan takes it personally as a statement about his life situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ryan moment - his narcissism and self-pity in just five words. Great economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Coach
dialogue.line: You guys are performing way below your potential. You need to be better than this.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Wow. I mean, I came here to play basketball, not to be personally attacked.

jokes.text: Do you know if you're right or left-handed? Do you even know? What hand do you use to answer the phone?
jokes.setup: Trash talk during volleyball game
jokes.punchline: Increasingly basic questions implying Pam doesn't know fundamental things about herself
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure in trash talk. The phone question is a nice specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Opposing player
dialogue.line: Do you even know what volleyball is?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yes, of course I do.

dialogue.speaker: Opposing player
dialogue.line: Do you know which side of the net you're supposed to be on?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Um, this side?

dialogue.speaker: Opposing player
dialogue.line: Do you know your own name?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Pam.

dialogue.speaker: Opposing player
dialogue.line: Do you know what color that shirt is?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Blue?

jokes.text: Maybe I played a little in junior high. And in high school. Maybe a little in college. And went to volleyball camp most summers.
jokes.setup: Pam appearing to be bad at volleyball
jokes.punchline: Revealing she's actually extensively experienced, each addition more significant
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure with perfect pacing. Great character moment showing Pam's hidden competency.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I'm not really a volleyball person. I was never picked for teams in school.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's okay, Pam. Just give it your best shot.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I was captain of my volleyball team sophomore year.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: And I got a full scholarship to play in college.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: And I almost went pro, but I decided to pursue art instead.

jokes.text: Had to be part of the group.
jokes.setup: Scranton wins volleyball and celebrates
jokes.punchline: Stanley's resigned, sarcastic participation in team celebration
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stanley - reluctantly participating in office enthusiasm. Maximum character in minimum words.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: I'm not celebrating. I'm just standing here while everyone else celebrates around me.

jokes.text: Crystal cool.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if winging the sketch is cool
jokes.punchline: Holly's attempt at cool slang with 'crystal cool' instead of 'crystal clear'
jokes.explanation: Holly trying to match Michael's casual energy with mixed-up slang. Shows their similar awkward energy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Is winging the sketch cool?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's crystal cool.

jokes.text: Don't set it to yourself.
jokes.setup: Andy playing volleyball
jokes.punchline: Andy apparently setting the ball to himself, showing poor understanding of the sport
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's overenthusiasm leading to poor sports performance. Physical comedy implied.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I got it! I got it!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Mine!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Set!

jokes.text: You weren't even moving.
jokes.setup: Andy claims he twisted his ankle
jokes.punchline: Jim's observation that Andy was stationary when he got 'injured'
jokes.explanation: Jim's perfect deadpan calling out Andy's fake injury. Good economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, you twisted your ankle while standing still? How is that even possible?

jokes.text: I don't hear cheaters, tramps, or women who break my friend's heart.
jokes.setup: Rolph ignoring Holly's request for substitution
jokes.punchline: His increasingly dramatic list of people he doesn't hear, ending with the most specific
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from generic insults to specific personal grievance. Rolph's loyalty to Michael shown dramatically.

dialogue.speaker: Rolph
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I don't hear you. I don't hear anybody. I don't hear Jim. I don't hear Pam. I don't hear anyone named Kevin or anyone with a lazy eye. I especially don't hear Holly.

jokes.text: We have to convince Dunder and Mifflin to go back in time fix their parents.
jokes.setup: Brainstorming Back to the Future parody
jokes.punchline: Michael's confused understanding that they need to fix the founders' parents instead of their relationship
jokes.explanation: Michael's literal interpretation of Back to the Future plot. Shows his confused logic applied to business history.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, so we're doing a Back to the Future thing? Okay, okay, I got it. We need to go back in time and fix the founding fathers' parents so they get together.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, Michael, that's not—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm on a roll here. We're like Marty McFly, but instead of saving our parents' relationship, we're saving the country by making sure George Washington's mom and dad fall in love.

jokes.text: Dun-der, Dun-der, Dunder, Dunder, Dunder...
jokes.setup: Holly suggesting Jaws parody
jokes.punchline: Holly doing Jaws theme music but with 'Dunder' instead of 'duh'
jokes.explanation: Obvious but effective parody music. Shows Holly's willingness to commit to silly ideas.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Dun-der, dun-der, dun-der, dun-der, dun-der, dun-der, dun-der, dun-der!

jokes.text: They're gonna wipe their asses with your serves! Piss all over your faces!
jokes.setup: Team trash talk before corporate volleyball match
jokes.punchline: Meredith's increasingly crude and graphic threats
jokes.explanation: Perfect Meredith - taking trash talk to inappropriate extremes. Character integration saves crude humor.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: You better watch yourself out there. I've been training for this my whole life.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: I will destroy you. I will absolutely destroy you.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: I'm gonna spike that ball so hard it's gonna take out your kneecaps.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: And then I'm coming for you with a folding chair.

jokes.text: Are you ready to play Slumdunder Mifflinaire?
jokes.setup: Holly and Michael's game show parody presentation
jokes.punchline: Combining 'Slumdog Millionaire' with 'Dunder Mifflin' for their company history game
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture mashup. The portmanteau is clever enough though not brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Welcome to 'Slumdog Millionaire Dunder Mifflin!' where our employees answer questions about company history for cash prizes!

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: That's right, Michael. And our first contestant is...

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: For $100, how many reams of paper does Dunder Mifflin sell per quarter?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: You have three lifelines: phone a friend, ask the audience, or use your computer.

jokes.text: I was there! I was a tour guide at Dartmouth College!
jokes.setup: Holly correctly answers trivia about Dunder and Mifflin meeting
jokes.punchline: Her impossibly specific personal connection to company founders from the 1940s
jokes.explanation: Absurd claim that Holly was present for historical company founding. Good commitment to the bit.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: My great-grandfather actually knew Samuel Dunder. They met at a printer's convention in Atlantic City in 1941. And my grandmother's cousin was married to Wyle Furness, who was Furman's college roommate, and Furman was best friends with Paul Halpert's brother.

jokes.text: Robert Mifflin had undiagnosed depression, which 9 million americans suffer from, and is very treatable.
jokes.setup: Presenting company history as game show
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly turning suicide into PSA moment during entertainment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe moment - inappropriate mental health PSA in comedy sketch. Very uncomfortable and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: And that's the history of Dunder Mifflin! Now, moving right along...

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Did you know that suicide is the second leading cause of death among young adults? If you or someone you know is struggling, please call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-8255.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Anyway, let's give a big hand to our contestants!

jokes.text: $250... is more money than I've ever seen in my life.
jokes.setup: Game show format with Michael as contestant
jokes.punchline: Michael's dramatic overstatement about a small amount of money
jokes.explanation: Michael's dramatic overacting in their sketch. Shows his commitment to performance even when inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's it? That's all I won? I could buy like... a car. A really bad car. Like a Gremlin. A used Gremlin from 1987 with a paint job that looks like a crime scene.

jokes.text: I usually don't enjoy the theater. But this is delightful.
jokes.setup: David watching Michael and Holly's increasingly inappropriate sketch
jokes.punchline: David's polite comment right before the sketch goes completely off the rails
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - David's praise right before disaster. Great timing and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: That's very funny.

jokes.text: For $500,000, is it, A) Scranton?
jokes.setup: Game show asking which branch will close next
jokes.punchline: Holly inadvertently revealing confidential layoff information as game show question
jokes.explanation: The moment the sketch becomes disaster. Brilliant cringe writing - turning comedy into horror for the audience.

dialogue.speaker: Game Show Host
dialogue.line: And the question is: Which Dunder Mifflin branch will be closing next?

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Oh no.

jokes.text: David Wallace told me!
jokes.setup: Holly asks how Michael knew Buffalo would close
jokes.punchline: Michael cheerfully reveals he broke David's confidence in front of everyone
jokes.explanation: Peak Michael obliviousness - cheerfully revealing confidential information. Perfect character moment in disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: How did you know Buffalo would close?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, I may have mentioned to everyone in the office that David was thinking about closing Buffalo.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: You told everyone?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yeah, I thought it would be good to give them a heads up. You know, morale and all that.

jokes.text: You've gotta be kidding me! We're the best branch in the company!
jokes.setup: Buffalo employees learning they're being laid off
jokes.punchline: Their justified outrage and ironic claim about being the best branch
jokes.explanation: Natural human reaction that adds to the cringe. The irony of claiming to be best while being closed.

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo Branch Employee
dialogue.line: We're being laid off? This is insane!

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo Branch Employee
dialogue.line: We're the best branch in the company!

dialogue.speaker: Buffalo Branch Employee
dialogue.line: How can they close down the best performing office?

jokes.text: A little boy said me, 'Is my daddy gonna have a job by Christmas?' He's just thinking about his own gifts.
jokes.setup: Michael describing emotional conversation with child about layoffs
jokes.punchline: Michael's cynical interpretation that the child only cares about getting presents
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor showing Michael's inability to understand emotional complexity. Perfect character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I had to explain to my son why daddy's company is letting people go. It was a really tough conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: He looked up at me with these big eyes and said, 'Does this mean we're not getting a Xbox for Christmas?'

jokes.text: Corporate deserves to get its ass kicked.
jokes.setup: Question about whether they should still play volleyball after the layoff announcement
jokes.punchline: Dwight's aggressive channeling of anger into sports competition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight response - turning emotional situation into competitive aggression. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: We should absolutely still play volleyball. In fact, I insist on it. Layoffs are no excuse for weakness. We need to channel our anger, our despair, our sense of betrayal into pure competitive domination on that court.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will spike every ball like it personally fired me. I will dive for every point like my severance package depends on it. And I will crush whoever stands across that net from me.

jokes.text: What does one fiance plus one lover equal? Answer, one whore.
jokes.setup: Rolph making accounting joke during volleyball game
jokes.punchline: Crude math equation calling Pam a whore for being with Jim while engaged to Roy
jokes.explanation: Crude but structured joke. Rolph's loyalty to Michael drives inappropriate aggression toward Pam.

jokes.text: We can let you play with that foot.
jokes.setup: Pam injured her ankle and they're deciding if she can play
jokes.punchline: Corporate player's sarcastic 'permission' for injured player to continue
jokes.explanation: Decent sarcasm but not particularly clever. Shows competitive tension escalating.

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Player
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, you should definitely keep playing. It's not like you need both ankles to walk or anything.

jokes.text: I'm so mad that Pam got hurt!
jokes.setup: Pam leaves for hospital, Jim goes with her
jokes.punchline: Andy's transparently fake emotional outburst about losing their best player
jokes.explanation: Andy's obvious insincerity about caring for Pam vs. caring about winning. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Pam! Pam, come back! You can't leave me! You're our best player! I need you! This is a disaster! A complete and utter disaster!

jokes.text: No, it was a tough audience.
jokes.setup: Michael says their sketch went well before the Buffalo thing
jokes.punchline: Holly's defensive excuse about tough audience when they destroyed people's livelihoods
jokes.explanation: Good deflection humor showing Holly's inability to face their disaster. Relatable performer excuse.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Well, you know, it was a tough crowd. People were very upset about losing their jobs.

jokes.text: Good, because my next question was do you have a game to get back to?
jokes.setup: Pam asks to hurry because she has a game to get back to
jokes.punchline: Doctor's dry response that he was about to ask the same question
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man comedy from medical professional. Nice callback to Pam's urgency.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Can you hurry? I have a game to get back to.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I was about to ask you the same thing.

jokes.text: You guys should hit the road before... I close down another branch.
jokes.setup: Michael saying goodbye to Holly and AJ
jokes.punchline: Self-aware joke about his tendency to accidentally reveal confidential information
jokes.explanation: Great self-aware callback showing Michael has some recognition of his mistake. Dark humor well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Holly, AJ, I want you to know that everything we've discussed here today - and I mean everything - stays in this room. It's confidential. You have my word.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I would never accidentally mention it to someone, and then have them tell someone else, and then have it spread through the entire office like it always does.

jokes.text: I didn't find the perfect moment, because I think that today was about just having today.
jokes.setup: Michael's talking head about not confessing feelings to Holly
jokes.punchline: Michael's surprisingly mature philosophical rationalization
jokes.explanation: Rare moment of Michael showing genuine emotional maturity. Character growth moment with poetic phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, I think sometimes in life you meet someone and you just click. You know, you're not even looking for it, but there it is. It's like all of a sudden you become aware of how much better your life could be, and how that possibility is right there in front of you. But you know what? Sometimes the mature thing to do, the really adult thing to do, is to just... let it go. Because wanting something doesn't mean it's yours to have. And if you really care about that person, you want them to be happy. Even if that means... well, even if that means they're happy without you.

jokes.text: And then it's perfect. I'm in no rush.
jokes.setup: Michael describing his long-term view of his relationship with Holly
jokes.punchline: Michael's uncharacteristic patience and optimism about love
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character moment showing Michael's growth. The simple 'I'm in no rush' is perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, I'm not a patient man. But with Holly, I can wait. I can wait forever if I have to. Because when you know, you know. And I know.

jokes.text: How many people need to get hurt before we learn a valuable lesson? One? Two? Three? Four? Five? Six? Seven? Eight?
jokes.setup: Dwight arguing they shouldn't substitute for Pam
jokes.punchline: His dramatic counting that goes nowhere and teaches no lesson
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight escalation that builds to nothing. His dramatic speeches often lack substance but sound important.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: One, two, three. See? I'm counting. Do you know what I'm doing? I'm counting to show you how serious this is. Four, five, six. This is important. Seven, eight, nine. I could go to ten, but I won't, because that would prove my point even more, and I've already proven it enough.

jokes.text: Send in the subs.
jokes.setup: Dwight's dramatic speech about not giving up
jokes.punchline: His immediate capitulation after the phone call confirms Pam won't return
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Dwight's dramatic speech. Great economy and structure with immediate reversal.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will not give up. I will not surrender. I will fight until my last breath.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Nothing can break my resolve. Nothing can—

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Hello? Yes, this is he. Oh. Oh, I see. Well, thank you for calling.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've decided to step down.

tmdb_overview: At the company picnic, Michael and Holly are reunited for the first time since their split. The rest of the staff competes in games, such as the volleyball tournament.
insight: Escalation-driven finale spreads 57 jokes across picnic chaos without reaching elite status.
